## FINDINGS
VERDICT: REJECT
1. guard.py:15-19 — `enter()` only checks `self._depth == 0` before deciding whether to acquire the mutex, with no thread-ownership tracking; once any thread has entered, any other thread's `enter()` skips acquiring the mutex and just increments the shared counter, so multiple threads can run inside the "guarded" critical section simultaneously (mutual exclusion is broken).
2. guard.py:26-31 — `exit()` has no validation that a matching `enter()` occurred; an unbalanced `exit()` call drives `_depth` negative and silently returns instead of raising, whereas the previous implementation raised `RuntimeError` on releasing an unlocked lock, so misuse is now masked rather than surfaced.
